Who we are

Meet Brad Skiles

CLU · ChFC · CFP® · CLTC
Investment Representative
Full Profile ↗ brad.skiles@cspwired.com

Brad joined what is now the Financial Design Group in 1989 and founded Center Street Partners at the end of 2011. He values the synergy that comes from independent advisors choosing to work together, creating better outcomes for business owners and their clients alike.

Preserving the purchasing power of wealth is one of Brad's most important objectives. He has been influenced by Austrian Economics, monetary history, and economic cycles, and brings that lens to long-term financial planning for individuals, business owners, and young executives and couples.

"The bottom line is that Brad helps people make smart decisions about their money, aligning their financial goals with the foundational values by which they live. That harmony can create financial confidence."

Brad's business life is blended with his faith and serving others. He has a weekly mentoring role with young ministry leaders, including overseas ministries working to change cultures. He has no plans to retire; he simply loves what he does.

Who we are

Meet Jay Hinkel

Financial Advisor
Full Profile ↗ jay.hinkel@cspwired.com

Jay Hinkel is a financial advisor with Financial Design Group, bringing a fresh perspective shaped by an unconventional and diverse professional background. Originally from Michigan, Jay now proudly calls Winona Lake home, where he lives with his wife and newborn daughter.

Before entering the financial advisory world, Jay built experience across business analytics in the orthopedic industry, occupational therapy, and asset management in the digital asset space. This cross-disciplinary foundation gives him a distinctive lens on risk, inflation, and long-term value creation, grounded in data, adaptability, and real-world problem-solving.

"True financial freedom isn't simply about growing wealth; it's about preserving it through wisdom and stewardship."

When he's not working with clients, Jay can be found training in jiu-jitsu, refereeing rugby games, and reading about the principles that underpin lasting financial health, always looking for better ways to align his clients' futures with sound monetary values.

How we work

Our Approach

At Center Street Partners, we believe great financial decisions aren't made in isolation. They're made in conversation, with someone who takes the time to understand not just your numbers, but your values, your family, and your vision for the future.

01

We Start With You

Every plan we build begins with your story, not a template.

02

Values-Aligned Planning

We align your financial plan with the values and vision that matter most to you.

03

Preserving What You've Built

We place equal emphasis on protecting your wealth as we do on growing it.

04

A Partnership, Not a Transaction

We build lasting relationships grounded in trust, transparency, and honest communication.

05

Independent & Objective

As an independent firm, our recommendations are driven by one thing: what is genuinely best for you.

06

Together We Decide

We walk through every decision with you so you always feel informed and confident in the path ahead.

What we focus on

Areas of Specialty

We bring deep knowledge and hands-on experience to investment areas that many advisors overlook. Our specialty focus allows us to help clients build resilient, diversified portfolios anchored in real, tangible value.

Gold & Silver

Foundational monetary metals that serve as proven hedges against inflation and currency debasement.

Precious Metals

We help clients explore platinum, palladium, and beyond as part of a diversified, inflation-resistant portfolio.

Energy

We help clients gain strategic exposure to oil, gas, and renewable energy markets with a long-term perspective.

Digital Assets

We bring grounded, informed guidance to cryptocurrency and blockchain investments for clients ready to explore this evolving space.

Donor Advised Funds

We help clients give wisely through tax-efficient charitable vehicles designed to maximize both impact and financial benefit.

Life Insurance & Annuities

We help clients protect their families and secure predictable retirement income through properly structured life insurance and annuity solutions.

Articles

Legacy Builders  ·  April 10, 2026  ·  #22

Why Finance Is Just As Important As Faith and Family

By Brad Skiles, CLU · ChFC · CFP® · CLTC

Every culture rests on a three-legged stool: Faith, Family, and Finance. All three matter, but they don't matter in the same way. Faith shapes eternity. Family shapes identity. And Finance, though often misunderstood, shapes the stability of everything else.

Most people instinctively defend the first two legs. We speak up when faith is threatened. We rally when family is under pressure. But the Finance leg? That one is usually ignored until it cracks. And when it cracks, the whole stool wobbles.

Legacy Builders understand this: you cannot build a strong culture on a weak monetary foundation. The most basic element of Finance is our means of exchange — our money.

Why the Finance Leg Matters More Than We Think

If you lived alone on an island, you wouldn't need money. But the moment two or more people begin exchanging goods, services, or labor, a medium of exchange becomes essential. Money is simply the tool a community uses to trade with fairness and efficiency.

That's why the Bible speaks so directly about it. When God gave Moses the laws for a new nation, He didn't just address worship, morality, or family life. He addressed commerce. He addressed money. He addressed the integrity of the marketplace:

"Do not use dishonest standards when measuring length, weight, or volume… Your scales and weights must be accurate."
"Always use honest weights and measures, so that you may enjoy a long life in the land the LORD your God is giving you."

Solomon echoed the same truth:

"The LORD detests the use of dishonest scales… He demands accurate scales and balances."

Why such strong language? Because dishonest money destroys trust, and trust is the oxygen of any culture. When a nation's money becomes unreliable, everything built on top of it begins to erode — business, family stability, generosity, and even the ability to defend faith in the public square.

When Money Breaks, Culture Breaks

Imagine this: you're fighting to protect religious liberty, strengthen families, or support your church. But suddenly the money in your bank account loses its value. Overnight, your focus shifts from cultural engagement to basic survival.

Food. Clothing. Shelter. Phone bills. Gas. Electricity.

Every one of these requires money. If the money collapses, the culture collapses with it.

This is not theoretical. It's historical. Since 1900, at least 57 currencies have collapsed through hyperinflation, usually triggered by war, political chaos, or uncontrolled money printing. Famous examples include Weimar Germany, Hungary, Zimbabwe, and Venezuela. These collapses share common warning signs: soaring debt, negative real rates, and rapid monetary expansion that destroys trust and stability.

And when money fails, culture follows. Families fracture under financial stress. Moral values erode as survival instincts take over. Trust in institutions collapses. Faith either weakens under fear or strengthens in small, resilient communities. When money fails, honesty, stability, and long-term thinking disappear.

Ernest Hemingway once wrote that bankruptcy comes "gradually, then suddenly." The same happens with currency failure. It starts slowly. Many people don't notice the erosion. Money doesn't buy as much as it used to. Packages shrink while prices stay the same.

You see it everywhere. A restaurant that once used high-quality oil now reuses it twice as long. "Made from scratch" restaurants begin using packaged frozen food. Why? Costs are rising and margins are shrinking. Quality declines because money declines.

Appliances that once lasted decades now fail in five years. I recently used a washing machine at my mom's home in California that was nearly as old as me — and still running. Meanwhile, Patti and I replaced our expensive refrigerator after only five years. Manufacturing shortcuts are often a response to inflationary pressure.

These everyday examples reveal a deeper truth: when the value of money declines, the quality of goods and services declines with it. Our culture suffers.

The Legacy Builders Call

Legacy Builders are not doomsayers. We are stewards. We are watchmen. We are people who understand that faithfulness in small things creates stability in big things.

Faith matters. Family matters. And money — honest money — matters just as much.

A culture cannot stand when one leg is weak. A legacy cannot endure when one pillar is ignored. If we want to build something that lasts — for our children, our churches, and our communities — we must strengthen all three legs of the stool, not just the ones we're comfortable talking about.

Faith shapes our eternity. Family shapes our identity. Finance shapes our stability. And stability is what allows a culture to flourish.

Disclosure: The opinions voiced in this material are for general information only and are not intended to provide specific advice or recommendations for any individual. Bradley K. Skiles, CLU, ChFC, CFP, CLTC is an LPL Registered Representative and Investment Representative. Securities and Advisory Services are offered through LPL Financial, a Registered Investment Advisor. Member FINRA/SIPC. Center Street Partners is independently owned and operated. Tracking #1088814, April 10, 2026.

Past Articles
Center Street Partners  ·  April 9, 2026

Meet Jay Hinkel!

In the spring of 2013, I met Jay Hinkel as a Grace College senior. It was his turn to sit across from me, and I asked him questions like, "Why do you want this job?" Little did either of us know where that meeting would lead.

Dear clients and friends,

Jay Hinkel

A few weeks after that first meeting, Jay called and came to my office. Although he was a graduating business major, I had a way of turning his world upside down. He became a student of monetary history, and that eventually led him into the world of cryptocurrencies.

Jay began investing in junior high. He and his sisters maintained a produce booth at a Farmer's Market in Michigan. Jay's dad was using Larry Burkett's financial principles with his three children. As the kids earned money — eventually, in high school, Jay worked in a hardware store — he was taught to give away 10% and save 50%. Except, instead of saving it was investing. Early stock purchases were Stryker and Apple. Eventually, Dave Hinkel, Jay's dad, left teaching school to become a financial advisor with Prudential. He then passed away in 2018. By watching his dad's life, Jay learned the benefit of good planning and caring for a family.

After college, Jay continued to save and invest, building a personal portfolio through swing trading. He has studied technical analysis and often has trading software open on his personal computer tracking market data. He brings a fresh and valuable perspective to Center Street Partners.

I've been emphasizing that my focus is fundamental analysis. I've studied this for 16 years. Jay and I have great discussions. His technical analysis complements my fundamental focus. He knows a lot about cryptocurrencies; I know a lot about precious metals. Jay has also invested his personal wealth in precious-metal strategies, as I have. We share a common view of the global economy and the challenges ahead. We make a good team.

I am not retiring. At age 69 (this summer), my goal is to help accelerate Jay's ability to work with his friends and clients. Jay will be 36 this year. He and his wife, Mary, welcomed their daughter, Esther, into their family last November. They enjoy living at Winona Lake, and Jay is active at Winona Grace Church. He also enjoys jiu-jitsu, refereeing rugby games, and reading financial topics.

How Jay Joined Center Street Partners

Jay will be helping me serve some of my clients, but most of his focus will be building his own client base and business. For years, I've had a goal of having someone like Jay involved in my business by 2027. It wasn't a plan when he contacted me last summer to ask if we could meet. He reminded me of our first meeting in 2013.

His career path — business analytics in the orthopedic industry, occupational therapy, and asset management in the digital-asset space — gave him broad experience and helped him identify that his true passion is helping people with financial advice. When Jay and I met last summer, we both assumed we were simply going to discuss the economy and monetary concerns. After two hours, I asked him to pray about joining me. He completed his investment licensing in February 2026 and officially began with me on Monday, February 16, 2026.

Jay considered other traditional financial firms as he began this career. Any of them likely would have offered him more than I did. I offered him nothing but office space and my experience. Within Center Street Partners and LPL Financial, he found the network of financial resources that aligned with his passion and investment views.

"I can't in good conscience recommend investments to clients that I don't believe will protect them from the changes coming. What I've found with you matches my desires for what I want to do personally and what I want to share with others." — Jay Hinkel

Looking Ahead Together

The financial services career is about serving people. Jay shares my concern that our national debt is an issue that makes the future different from the past. We're not going to be replaced by artificial intelligence. There is a need for contrarian thinking. What I've been thinking about for more than 30 years is now happening. The rest of this decade is going to be difficult to navigate. Wrong decisions here may have generational impact.

Too many financial advisors focus too much on the past rather than the future. Many have not been trained to see the potholes in the road ahead. If you've benefited from my advice, perhaps you may be able to introduce friends or family to Jay. Because we want more people in lifeboats. Our financial-lifeboat strategy has the potential to grow wealth while also protecting purchasing power.

This is an important time to use the right financial strategies as our world experiences massive change and risk. Let's do this together.

With many regards, — Brad  ·  Psalm 20:7

Legacy Builders · April 3, 2026 · #21

Ancient Wisdom for Honest Money

Every generation feels like it's the first to face economic uncertainty. Long before Wall Street or central banks, a set of principles was written around 1445 B.C. that spoke directly to the heart of monetary integrity.

Were these ancient rules meant to guide every culture — including ours?

To explore that, we need to look at the earliest days of trade. According to NOVA, China was using metal money around 1000 B.C. But even earlier, in Moses' time, commerce depended on weights and measurements. Trade only worked if everyone agreed on the standards.

So, when Moses wrote, "Do not use dishonest standards… your scales and weights must be accurate," he wasn't offering a religious suggestion. He was establishing the economic foundation of a nation. And he wasn't alone. Over the next two centuries, multiple biblical authors repeated the same theme: Honest weights. Honest measures. Honest means of exchange.

It was the backbone of a stable society.

The Power of Fixed Measurements

Our entire culture still depends on fixed units: 12 inches in a foot, 3 feet in a yard, 16 ounces in a pound, 32,000 ounces in a ton. The U.S. Constitution empowers Congress to fix weights and measures. (See Article I, Section 8, Clause 5.)

Imagine watching a football game where the referees quietly changed the definition of a yard. Or playing Monopoly with your grandkids and the eight-year-old banker keeps raising the price of every property. You'd quit. Everyone would. Standards matter.

And yet, while we would never tolerate changing inches or ounces, we've accepted a monetary system where the value of a dollar changes every second of every day. The dollar — our national unit of economic measurement — is no longer fixed. It floats. It drifts. It erodes.

Most Americans alive today have never lived under a gold standard. We've only known a fiat dollar, a currency created at will, with no intrinsic value behind it. When we trade our labor for dollars, we assume those dollars will hold the value of our work. But when money can be created "out of thin air," the measurement becomes dishonest — even if unintentionally so.

And dishonest measurements always hurt the people who can least afford it.

Back to 1400 B.C. — A Nation Built on Honest Measures

When Israel left Egypt and formed a new nation, God gave them laws that shaped everything from worship to economics. Even if someone doesn't believe the Bible, it's remarkable how clear and consistent these monetary principles were.

Deuteronomy 25:13–15 (NLT)
"You must use accurate scales when you weigh out merchandise, and you must use full and honest measures. Yes, always use honest weights and measures, so that you may enjoy a long life in the land the LORD your God is giving you."

The message is unmistakable: a society cannot thrive when its measurements are corrupt. The prophets condemned merchants who mixed grain with chaff or used rigged weights to cheat the poor. Proverbs calls dishonest scales "detestable." The Levites were even assigned to check the weights and measures — an ancient version of the Bureau of Standards. This wasn't just about economics. It was about trust, justice, and the dignity of every person.

Were These Principles Meant for Us?

Our Founding Fathers thought so. They lived through the collapse of colonial paper money and the disaster of the Continental Dollar. They saw firsthand what happens when a nation manipulates its own currency. So when they wrote the Constitution, they intentionally tied money to precious metals — a standard that couldn't be printed, inflated, or manipulated.

Their warnings sound almost prophetic now:

  • George Washington: "Depreciation keeps pace with the quantity of the emission."
  • George Mason: "Paper money is founded upon fraud and knavery."
  • Oliver Ellsworth: "Shut and bar the door against paper money."
  • Thomas Jefferson: "The end of democracy… will occur when government falls into the hands of lending institutions."
  • Andrew Jackson, confronting the central bank: "You are a den of vipers and thieves."

These weren't fringe voices. These were the architects of our nation. They believed honest money was essential for a free people.

The Legacy Builder's Question

So here we are — thousands of years after Moses, hundreds of years after the Founders — still wrestling with the same issue: can a society flourish when its measurements are constantly changing?

Legacy Builders know the answer. Whether you're running a business, leading a family, or stewarding resources, the principles are the same:

  • Integrity in small things creates stability in big things.
  • Honest measurements build trust.
  • Trust builds nations.

The ancient world understood it. The Founding Fathers understood it. And deep down, we know it too. There's a reason we call it "honest money."

Because legacies — personal or national — are built on truth, not manipulation. On fixed standards, not shifting ones. On faithfulness, not convenience. And that's the kind of foundation that endures.

Tracking #1085390, April 3, 2026.

Gold Report  ·  March 18, 2026

Wars and Rumors of Wars

The new conflict with Iran has raised understandable questions about markets, gold, and what investors should expect next. Several key fundamentals that drive precious metals are being directly affected, yet short-term price weakness has come from the futures market — not the underlying story.

Dear clients and friends,

Jay Hinkel

If we could rewind to Friday, February 27, 2026, and we had inside knowledge that the U.S. was about to begin a war with Iran, what would be the right investment position? I asked this question to ChatGPT, and here is its answer:

"If you knew the U.S. was about to start a war with Iran, the best trade would be long oil, defense stocks, and gold, and short airlines and travel. Middle East conflicts often spike oil prices (Hormuz risk), boost military spending, and trigger safe-haven buying."

I thought it would have said "dollars." Dollars have been the primary safe-haven position for times like these. As I write this, the dollar has gained about 3% during this period. For many of my clients, we've had a strong position in energy stocks for a number of years — that has been profitable so far this year.

10 Fundamental Factors Driving Gold

Here are the 10 fundamental factors that I believe have driven the price of gold since the year 2000. Consider how many are impacted by this war:

  1. Shrinking supply of new gold
  2. Growing demand from central banks
  3. Growing demand from consumers in developing countries
  4. Global fiscal and monetary policies
  5. Reckless U.S. government spending
  6. Outstanding derivatives yet to be valued
  7. Geopolitical risks
  8. Minimal U.S. investor ownership
  9. Small trading volumes for gold and silver
  10. Growing recognition of manipulation of paper contracts

#7 — Geopolitical risks. "War" is the worst risk. So, the fact that we have a war is generally viewed as fuel for higher gold prices. Then why hasn't the gold price increased? Hold that thought.

#5 — Reckless U.S. government spending. Some reports estimate our war costs at $1 billion per day. Part of the problem is Iranian drones costing $20,000–$50,000 are being shot down with $3–$5 million interceptor missiles. Prior to this war, military spending for this year was planned at $1 trillion. ChatGPT gives a range of $100B to $500B for the increase in war spending and rearmament. As I write this, our national debt is $38.9 trillion. Wars ALWAYS require more spending. Since the Revolutionary War, wars have always created money printing.

#4 — Global fiscal and monetary policies. The monetary policy of the U.S. is to print more money. Our political class knows they cannot stay in office by raising taxes to pay for a war. So they give us a stealth tax — a tax not seen on our radar screen. By printing money, the currency loses purchasing power and we pay a tax called "inflation." The classical definition of inflation is: "an increase in the money supply, which leads to higher prices." Inflation is not a mystery. It is a government plan.

#10 — Growing recognition of manipulation of paper contracts. Andy Schectman, owner of Miles Franklin, says the withdrawal activity at the COMEX is the equivalent of four feet of snow in Death Valley. With more than 30 years of precious metals experience, he says he has never seen this volume of bullion leaving the COMEX vaults. He concludes that "big money" is taking physical delivery of bullion to be held outside the system.

Why Have Precious Metals Declined?

First, let's acknowledge we are at the highest level for both gold and silver for any March in history. Gold has what looks like a solid bottom at $5,000. The weakness in prices has been created in the futures (paper) market — not the underlying story. The physical market is strong.

I do not think these first two weeks are an indicator of future momentum. I think we are still on track. Perhaps we've seen the low for this year. Maybe not. That is why this is called "investment risk."

Introducing Jay Hinkel

I have a new associate helping me — Jay Hinkel. Together we plan to call clients when we see momentum changing. We want to discuss allocation changes. We are not suggesting leaving precious metals investments entirely.

I first met Jay in 2013 when he was a senior at Grace College. He graduated and had a variety of experiences that have prepared him for his new career. We are both excited about our teamwork. I am not retiring. I have no intention of stopping what I'm doing. This is a very good move for my business.

The new war leads me to think this theme of precious metals investments and hard assets may take us through this decade. The peak may not happen until after 2030. This is a bull market that is not yet done — that's what I think. We will continue to watch this together.

— Brad Skiles, Psalm 20:7

Privacy Policy

Last updated: April 2026

This Privacy Policy describes how Center Street Partners ("we," "us," or "our") collects, uses, and protects the personal information of clients and visitors to our website. Please review this policy carefully. By using our website or engaging our services, you agree to the practices described below.

1. Information We Collect

We may collect personal information you voluntarily provide, including your name, email address, phone number, and financial information necessary to provide advisory services. We may also collect non-personal browsing data through standard web analytics tools.

2. How We Use Your Information

We use your information to provide financial planning and investment advisory services, communicate with you about your account and our services, comply with applicable legal and regulatory requirements, and improve our website and client experience.

3. Information Sharing

We do not sell, trade, or rent your personal information to third parties. We may share information with LPL Financial as our broker-dealer and registered investment advisor, regulatory bodies as required by law, and service providers who assist in our operations under strict confidentiality agreements.

4. Data Security

We implement reasonable administrative, technical, and physical safeguards to protect your personal information from unauthorized access, disclosure, or misuse. However, no method of transmission over the internet is completely secure.

5. Your Rights

You may request access to, correction of, or deletion of your personal information at any time by contacting us at brad.skiles@cspwired.com. We will respond to all reasonable requests in accordance with applicable law.

6. Cookies

Our website may use cookies to enhance your browsing experience. You may disable cookies in your browser settings, though this may affect certain site functionality.

7. Contact Us

For questions about this Privacy Policy, please contact:
